title: Function Declaration
description: The function prototype: telling the compiler a function exists before its full body is written, so it can be called earlier in the file.
objectives: Understand what a function declaration (prototype) is
Know why a declaration is needed when a function is defined after where it's called
Write a function prototype with correct syntax
Connect this back to the Declaration vs Definition idea from Header Files

sections.text: Back in Header Files, we learned: a declaration tells the compiler that something exists; a definition provides the actual implementation. Functions follow exactly this same split.
2. The Problem: Compiler Reads Top to Bottom

The compiler processes a file from top to bottom. When it reaches add(5, 3) inside main(), it hasn't seen add defined yet — as far as it knows at that point, add doesn't exist. This causes a compilation error.

sections.code: int add(int a, int b); // declaration (prototype)

sections.text: A function declaration — also called a prototype — tells the compiler "a function named add exists, it takes two ints, and it returns an int" without providing the actual body yet. That's enough for the compiler to accept a call to add() before it has seen the full definition.
A function declaration states a function's return type, name, and parameter types, telling the compiler it exists without providing its body.

sections.title: What changed
points: int add(int a, int b); appears before main() — the compiler now knows add exists
main() can call add(5, 3) even though the actual definition comes later in the file
The real definition, with its body, still appears further down

sections.text: 5. Parameter Names Are Optional in a Declaration

sections.code:
int add(int, int); // also valid — names dropped
int add(int a, int b); // also valid — names kept for readability

sections.text: A declaration only needs the types the compiler will check calls against. Parameter names in a declaration are purely for a human reader's benefit and don't have to match the names used in the actual definition.
6. Why This Matters for Header Files

This connects directly back to Header Files: a .h file commonly holds function declarations (like int add(int, int);), while the matching .cpp file holds the actual definitions. Other files can #include the header and call the function, without needing to see (or recompile) its implementation.

sections.title: A table of contents
sections.text: A book's table of contents tells you "Chapter 5: The Ocean" exists and roughly what it covers, before you've actually read it. A function declaration is that table-of-contents entry — a promise that the full chapter (the definition) exists somewhere in the book, even if you haven't reached that page yet.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Calling a function before it's defined, with no declaration above the call — causes a compilation error, since the compiler hasn't "met" that name yet.
Writing a declaration whose types don't match the actual definition — the parameter types and return type must agree between the two.
Forgetting the semicolon at the end of a declaration — int add(int, int) without a ; is not a valid standalone declaration.
Thinking a declaration and a definition are the same thing — a declaration has no body; only the definition does.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a function declaration?
questions.answer: A statement that tells the compiler a function's return type, name, and parameter types, without providing its body — also called a function prototype.

questions.question: Why is a function declaration needed?
questions.answer: Because the compiler reads a file top to bottom; if a function is called before its full definition appears, a declaration earlier in the file lets the compiler accept the call.

questions.question: Do parameter names need to appear in a function declaration?
questions.answer: No — a declaration only requires the parameter types; names are optional and purely for readability.

questions.question: How does function declaration relate to header files?
questions.answer: Header files commonly contain function declarations, while the matching source file contains the definitions — letting other files call the function after including the header, without needing its implementation.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A function declaration, or prototype, tells the compiler a function's return type, name, and parameter types without providing its body — for example, int add(int, int);. It's needed because the compiler reads a file from top to bottom, so calling a function before its full definition appears would otherwise be an error. This is the same declaration vs definition split we saw with header files: a header commonly holds function declarations, while the matching .cpp file holds the actual implementations.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Function Declaration
sections.text: Declaration = a promise it exists (table of contents). Definition = the actual chapter. The compiler just needs the promise to accept a call earlier in the file.
